topic: The Breakthrough That Created Modern Computers
title: How ENIAC Opened the Door to Modern Computing

lesson.learn:
Modern computers began when engineers proved that electronic machines could be general-purpose and programmable. ENIAC showed that a computer could do many kinds of calculations by changing instructions instead of rebuilding hardware each time. That idea turned computing into a flexible tool rather than a one-off machine.

The leap mattered because speed was only part of the story. ENIAC also helped establish the pattern that still defines computing today: inputs go in, logic processes them, and useful results come out quickly enough to matter in the real world.
lesson.simpler: ENIAC proved that computers could be electronic, fast, and flexible. Instead of one machine for one job, engineers could build systems that handled many tasks.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Modern computing started when electronics made computers programmable instead of fixed-purpose.
lesson.deeperDive:
ENIAC was built with thousands of vacuum tubes, which made it huge, power-hungry, and difficult to maintain. Even so, it was much faster than earlier mechanical calculators. The deeper breakthrough was not only the hardware, but the way it changed expectations: a computer could be reconfigured for new problems, and later systems would improve that idea with stored programs, transistors, integrated circuits, and microprocessors.

That path explains why computers shrank from room-sized machines to devices we now carry everywhere. The core logic stayed the same, but the components became smaller, cheaper, cooler, and more reliable.
lesson.funFact: ENIAC filled a room and still had to be rewired by hand.
references: University of Pennsylvania - Moore School computing history

people: J. Presper Eckert - co-inventor of ENIAC
John von Neumann - advanced stored-program ideas
Ted Hoff - helped usher in the microprocessor era
places: Moore School of Electrical Engineering, Philadelphia - ENIAC development site
University of Pennsylvania - wartime computing research
Silicon Valley - later home of the microprocessor boom
concepts: Programmability - changing instructions without rebuilding hardware
Microprocessor - CPU on a single chip
General-purpose computing - one machine for many tasks
